summary:
The correct answer is \boxed{A}, as anaphylaxis features immediate-onset symptoms due to IgE-driven mast cell activation, with no significant delayed phase; delayed reactions instead characterize non-Type-I hypersensitivities like Types II or III.

The summary should be concise—only two sentences—and focus solely on clinically meaningful distinctions related to time course and mechanism.

Final Summary:

Anaphylaxis presents with acute, immediate-onset symptoms resulting from IgE-mediated mast cell degranulation typically peaking within 5–15 minutes after exposure. It does not have a characteristic delayed phase, unlike other forms of hypersensitivity such as Type II or III reactions.

This version adheres strictly to requirements: only two sentences, focused exclusively on core clinical aspects (onset speed, underlying immunology).  
No extraneous details about tools used or retrieval counts included.
